# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe ISD School Safety and Security Committee Meeting
### April 24, 2024 — Regular Meeting

> **Note:** These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions. They are not a substitute for official board minutes.

---

## Call to Order
The meeting was called to order at 4:00 PM on April 24, 2024.

---

## Item 1: Approval of Minutes
**Action:** Minutes from the previous Safety and Security meeting (November 2023) were considered for approval.

**Vote:** Motion made and seconded. Approved unanimously with no opposition.

---

## Item 2: Cell Phone Safety Awareness Initiative

**Presenter:** Ms. Rodriguez (and Dr. Medford for cell phone guidelines overview)

### Key Points:
- Based on recommendations from the SHAC (School Health Advisory Council), the district implemented several cell phone safety awareness efforts:
  - **Ongoing social media campaign** educating schools and parents about cell phone safety tips
  - **Community presentations** equipping parents with tools to navigate the digital age
  - **Cell Phone Safety Week** planned for the 2024–2025 school year (similar to Suicide Prevention Week and Red Ribbon Week), led by school counselors to empower students with responsible cell phone usage

### Cell Phone Guidelines (approved by the Board):
- **PreK–6th grade:** Cell phones off and away for the entire school day
- **Junior High:** Cell phones away all day except during lunch and passing periods (campus discretion on specifics)
- **High School:** Cell phones away except for clear instructional purposes

- Junior high and high school guidelines were rolled out at the semester break
- **Positive feedback** received from teachers and parents
- Community surveys were conducted during the development process to gather parent input
- End-of-year feedback from faculty and community will be gathered to refine guidelines for next year

**Discussion:** The chair noted this was a great example of the system working — an idea originating from the SHAC, flowing to the board, going through a large committee process, and being implemented in buildings. Guidelines are a starting point and may evolve over time.

---

## Item 3: Artificial Intelligence Software Exploration

**Presenters:** Ethan (Safety staff) and Chief Block

### Background:
- Conroe ISD has **4,500+ cameras** with approximately **12,000+ camera views/channels**
- Five AI vendors were evaluated for potential integration with the district's camera systems

### Vendors Reviewed:

| Vendor | Key Details | Pricing |
|--------|------------|---------|
| **21st Century AI** | 90% camera coverage integration | $7.8 million upfront; 10% annual maintenance for 5 years; $10,000 per campus installation |
| **Omnilert** | Initial meeting held; vendor stopped responding after January 5 | No final pricing received |
| **Actuate** | Pricing per camera per month | ~$18/camera/month (weapons detection only); estimated ~$500,000+/year for district |
| **Zero Eyes** | Tiered pricing by camera streams | 5-year: $2.6M annually; 3-year: $3.15M annually; plus $15,000 platform fee (possibly per campus) |
| **Drift Net** (proprietary) | Installs own camera hardware; includes visitor management (could replace Raptor); claims ability to detect edged weapons/knives | $400,000–$550,000/year; 3-year contract: $1.24M–$1.7M |

### Key Findings & Concerns:
- **All systems require a weapon to be visibly brandished** — they cannot detect concealed weapons (in backpacks, under clothing, etc.)
- Four of the five vendors offer substantially similar technology; Drift Net is the proprietary outlier
- Systems can generate **false positives** from BB guns, airsoft guns, toy weapons, etc.
- **A human element is always required** — alerts must be vetted by a person before response
- By the time AI detects a brandished weapon, the threat is already active — these systems **do not prevent attacks**, they enable faster notification and response
- **Network concerns** raised about Drift Net's proprietary cameras potentially taxing the district's network
- **Data privacy concerns** raised regarding who owns/stores video when using external camera systems, particularly with students involved

### Weapons Detection Systems (walk-through type):
- Brief discussion of weapons detection systems (similar to those at Astros games)
- Estimated cost: ~$20,000 per movable unit
- Significant **staffing requirements** — each unit needs dedicated personnel, ideally police officers
- Challenge of large high schools with 120+ exterior doors noted

**No action taken — informational item only.**

---

## Item 4: Staying Safe School Safety Response Training (Texas School Safety Center)

**Presenters:** Ethan and Chief Block

### Background:
- At the committee's direction, Ethan and Chief Block attended the "Staying Safe" training at the Region 6 Service Center in Huntsville, provided by the Texas School Safety Center.

### Key Findings:

- The curriculum aims to **empower students** in active shooter response situations
- Uses familiar framework (Run/Hide/Fight or Avoid/Deny/Defend) but **changes "Defend" to "Disrupt"**
- The "Disrupt" concept would be introduced at the **intermediate and junior high levels and up** (not elementary)
- The curriculum encourages **students to think critically and make independent decisions** during a crisis
- Would facilitate **small group discussions** where teachers guide students on how to respond, including the "fight/disrupt" option

### Concerns Raised:
- **Language change** from "Defend" to "Disrupt" could create confusion with the existing Standard Response Protocol
- The presenter themselves **inconsistently used terminology**, creating confusion during the training
- **Does not include all five phases of emergency management** — focused only on response, not prevention or mitigation
- **Does not include parent involvement** — many parents would likely have strong opinions about their children being taught these concepts
- Concern about **asking too much of teachers** — could become traumatic for staff and students even in the training context
- **Cognitive ability concerns** — questioning whether intermediate-age students can appropriately analyze crisis situations and make independent decisions
- **Risk of independent student action** potentially conflicting with teacher instructions and endangering others (e.g., a student trying to flee while a teacher directs lockdown)
- Other districts at the training table **shared similar reservations**
- The program may be better suited for districts without robust existing safety infrastructure (e.g., rural districts without dedicated police departments)

### Recommendation:
- Presenters and committee expressed that **this program is not a good fit for Conroe ISD at this time** given its infancy
- May warrant revisiting in 4–5 years after further development and implementation elsewhere
- Some elements could potentially be incorporated into existing training

### Mandatory Status:
- The Texas School Safety Center made clear this training is **voluntary, not mandatory**, and there is **no indication it will become mandatory**

**No formal action taken — informational item.**

---

## Item 5: Intruder Detection Audits for 2023–2024 School Year

**Presenter:** Mr. McCord

### Key Points:
- This is the **second year** of intruder detection audits
- The district has **65 physical buildings**; **58 have been audited** as of the meeting date
- **7 remaining buildings** will be audited in May
- **Results: 57 out of 58 audited buildings had no findings** (98.27% success rate)
- The **one finding** occurred in November and was previously discussed (specifics reviewed in prior executive session)

### Notable Highlights:
- **No executive session was needed** for this item — all information could be shared in open session
- Results have **exceeded expectations** — described as "unbelievable" and "transformative"
- **Students have been exemplary** — not a single instance of a student letting an unauthorized person into a building through a door
- Credit given to students, staff, teachers, administrators, and maintenance crews for persistent effort despite the inconvenience
- Some of the district's campuses are compared to small cities in size (Conroe High School would be in the top quartile of Texas cities/towns by population)
- Final results will be reported to the Board of Trustees at the May board meeting
- Additional requirements from the state may come in fall 2024 (details still pending)

**No formal action taken — informational item.**

---

## Closing Remarks
- Committee members were encouraged to share ideas, concerns, or topics for future investigation with leadership at any time
- Reminder that items like the AI exploration originated from committee member suggestions

---

## Adjournment
**Motion to adjourn** made by Dr. Stickler. Meeting adjourned.
